draft.introduction: The Hymn to Viracocha is a Quechua prayer to the Inca creator god, preserved by Spanish chroniclers. Viracocha created the cosmos, set the sun and moon in motion, and fashioned humanity from stone at Tiahuanaco. The hymn’s theological sophistication surprised European observers.

translation.original_ref: Quechua oral tradition, recorded by Cristobal de Molina
translation.text: O Viracocha, lord of the universe, whether you are male, whether you are female, lord of reproduction, wherever you may be—in the upper world, in the lower world, on the sides of the world—creator of all things, maker of the people, lord of all lords, I search for you with straining eyes, and I cannot see you. I desire to know you, to understand you. You see me, you know me. The sun, the moon, the day, the night, spring and winter, are not in vain but are ordered—they move to the appointed place, they arrive at the appointed time.

translator_notes.note: The hymn’s address to a deity who may be “male or female” and who is searched for but not seen resonates with apophatic theology across traditions. The Inca concept of Pachayachachiq (“teacher of the world”) attributes pedagogical intent to creation itself—the cosmos is ordered not randomly but purposefully, as a form of divine instruction.
